01a_quotes.title: AI-Powered Philanthropy: Carryover Effects on Volunteer Engagement

study_design: We adopt a multi-method approach by first modeling how the introduction of AI affects a volunteer's utility and its implications for their future engagement.
Using a difference-in-differences (DID) design, we causally estimate the carryover effect of AI introduction on volunteer engagement after the adoption period.

We focus on carryover effects rather than direct effects during the adoption period period for two main reasons. First, the disaster response period involves higher volunteer activity due to the urgency of the disaster rather than typical volunteering motivations, making carryover effects a cleaner measure of AI's impact on future volunteer engagement.
Our quasi-natural experiment emerged during the Turkey-Syria earthquake in February 2023, when HOTTM faced an urgent need to process large amounts of mapping data quickly.
Because HOTTM set the default editor, volunteers contributing to these projects were externally introduced to Rapid, regardless of their prior editor preferences.
We define the period from February 6 to March 7, 2023, as the adoption period during which this systematic introduction of AI occurred.

To test the existence of the impact philanthropy effect among volunteers and to understand the effect of AI on them, we first create composite scores from participant responses in the third component of the experiment.
To address these challenges, we adopt a changes-in-changes (CIC) estimation approach using our full sample of 836 treated volunteers and 32,298 control volunteers.
task: HOTTM volunteers' primary task is to trace infrastructure (e.g., buildings, roads, waterways) on satellite imagery of the areas of interest using a mapping editor.

To contribute, volunteers log onto HOTTM and select a project that they want to work on. Upon clicking on a project's page, volunteers can then read a description of the project and what infrastructure needs to be mapped.
volunteers contribute to a project by selecting a task and tracing the required infrastructure (e.g., buildings, roads, waterways) on satellite imagery of the areas of interest within it.
The voluntary task asks participants to create a social media post for the NPO's outreach campaign. This task is unpaid and optional; participants may skip it and proceed to the third component.

Both conditions receive identical instructions: participants view sample social media posts demonstrating the desired content and writing style of the NPO. Next, participants are instructed to write a social media post to accompany an image about the NPO.
population: Between 2022 and 2023 alone, 645,000 volunteers contributed to 3,000 projects on the platform (HOT 2023).

We identify 2,803 volunteers who used Rapid for the first time during the adoption period. To causally measure how volunteer engagement changes after being introduced to Rapid, we retain only those 836 volunteers who made contributions to HOTTM before they ever used Rapid, that is, they contributed to the platform prior to the adoption period.

For our control group, we consider active volunteers who are not part of the treated group and contributed to HOTTM prior to the adoption period. We define an active volunteer as someone who has contributed to HOTTM within 431 days of February 6, 2023, a threshold that is three standard deviations longer than the average time in between volunteering sessions on HOTTM prior to February 6, 2023 (Ren et al. 2012, Urrea and Yoo 2023).
Our final dataset consists of 836 treated volunteers who performed 151,929 tasks and 32,298 control volunteers who performed 1,156,124 tasks during the observation window.

By applying CEM, we construct a control group that consists of volunteers who were not introduced to AI but demonstrate comparable engagement and experience on HOTTM to the treated volunteers who were introduced to AI, all measured prior to the adoption period. This matching process leads to a matched sample of 360 treated volunteers and 360 control volunteers.

We recruit 600 participants (49.5% female, Mage = 44.2, SDage = 13.5) for the online experiment. The average completion time is 10.9 minutes (i.e., an average hourly payment of $11.). Of these 600 participants, 108 participants (58.3% female, Mage = 46.0, SDage = 14.4) choose to do the volunteering task, and 55 participants are randomly assigned to the control condition and 53 to the AI-assisted condition.
arms: We define a treated volunteer as someone who used Rapid for the first time during the adoption period.
For our control group, we consider active volunteers who are not part of the treated group and contributed to HOTTM prior to the adoption period.

Control participants receive an empty text box to write the social media post from scratch. AI-assisted participants find the text box pre-filled with content generated using ChatGPT's GPT-4o free version, following the same instructions given to participants.
55 participants are randomly assigned to the control condition and 53 to the AI-assisted condition.
outcome_definitions: Our dependent variable, Tasksit, is operationalized as the number of tasks completed on HOTTM by volunteer i in month t. The number of tasks completed serves as a quantitative measure of volunteer participation and engagement on the platform (Burtch et al. 2013, Baruch et al. 2016, Urrea and Yoo 2023).

They indicate agreement with statements on a scale of 1-7 (1 = Strongly disagree; 7 = Strongly agree): "Volunteering my time makes a difference in the lives of the kids served by the Educational NPO," "Volunteering for the Educational NPO is an effective way for me to make a difference," "It is easy for me to imagine how volunteering my time will benefit the Educational NPO," "I put significant effort into helping the Educational NPO," and "I was thorough in helping the Educational NPO" (adapted from Duncan 2004, Buell et al. 2017, Mejia et al. 2019, Buell et al. 2021). These statements appear in random order to the participants. The first three statements capture perceived impact of volunteering while the last two assess the perceived effort.

We average the three perceived impact of volunteering statements into a single score called PIVi and the two perceived effort statements into PEi. Cronbach's alphas of 0.88 and 0.84, respectively, indicate high internal consistency.
statistical_results: Based on its negative and statistically significant estimate, we find that introduction to AI leads to a negative carryover effect (−0.240, p <0.01). Specifically, compared to a control volunteer, a treated volunteer reduces their monthly engagement by an average of 21.3%.
From the graph, we observe that the negative effect of AI persists across all months after the treatment.

The results support the parallel trends assumption, as none of the coefficients are statistically significant, confirming that treated and control volunteers follow similar engagement trends prior to AI introduction.

We find that AI-assisted volunteers report a lower perceived effort (4.6, S.E. = 0.237) compared to the control condition (5.6, S.E. = 0.150), and this difference is statistically significant ( p <0.01).

AI-assisted volunteers report a lower perceived impact of volunteering (5.0, S.E. = 0.206) compared to the control condition (5.5, S.E. = 0.151), and this difference is statistically significant ( p <0.05).

At the lower quantiles, representing less experienced volunteers, AI introduction has large negative effects on task contributions. Specifically, volunteers at the 20th percentile reduce their contributions by 19.99 tasks per week ( p <0.05), while those at the 30th, 40th, and 50th percentiles exhibit reductions of 21.37 ( p <0.01), 18.02 ( p <0.01), and 16.39 ( p <0.01) tasks after the adoption period, respectively.
The negative effect persists through the 60th percentile with a reduction of 7.34 tasks ( p <0.05).
At the 70th percentile, the effect becomes positive but statistically insignificant (2.32 tasks; p = 0.530).
volunteers at the 80th percentile increase contributions by 12.97 tasks (p <0.01), and those at the 90th percentile do so by 21.69 tasks ( p <0.01).
We estimate that after being introduced to AI, volunteers reduce their contributions by 21.3% on average.
In practical terms, this finding indicates that to maintain the volunteer workforce's average engagement prior to the introduction of AI, NPOs will need to recruit 27.1%5 more new volunteers.
ambiguity_or_caveats: Although volunteers could not distinguish this default setting until after selecting a project and reading the instructions, more active volunteers may have had a higher probability of encountering a Rapid-default project simply due to their greater participation.

That said, we acknowledge that the treatment may not be exogenous since volunteers did not receive treatment randomly. Instead, volunteers entered the treatment condition by choosing a project that had Rapid set as the default editor.

While this does not entirely eliminate the possibility of selection bias, it mitigates the concern because volunteers are effectively choosing projects without knowing prior knowledge of whether they would be prompted to use Rapid.

It is possible that once volunteers selected which projects to contribute to, they could choose not to use Rapid, even if it was set as the default editor. This is not a major concern, as OSM data reveal that the vast majority of treated volunteers (95.6%) in our sample used the default editor.

First, we only focus on volunteers being introduced to AI by utilizing Rapid to finish a task or a portion of a task at least once. Within a single task, volunteers could begin by using Rapid and then deactivate it for the remainder of the task. While the platform logs some information related to task duration, these measures are not sufficiently precise to capture how long volunteers actually spend using Rapid within each task. Our data do not provide insights into the proportion of time or effort within tasks that was performed with Rapid versus not.

Second, we do not account for the quality of the predicted suggestions by Rapid, and the quality of AI tools may impact AI adoption (Kwon et al. 2023). Unfortunately, the platform lacks objective quality measures, and currently, there is no available data to assess the quality of Rapid's predicted suggestions against such measures. Thus, it is possible that volunteers may react differently when introduced to AI tools performing much better or worse than the volunteers themselves. Given the current lack of data, AI quality's impact on volunteer engagement is beyond this project's scope, and we call on future research to explore this topic.
On HOTTM, experience is highly skewed — almost 99% of the volunteers have very little experience volunteering on the platform (Urrea and Yoo 2023).

When we apply our CEM procedure to create comparable treatment and control groups within experience categories, we lose substantial power due to the restrictive nature of matching across multiple variables on already small sub-samples.

01a_quotes.notes: This is an empirical study combining a quasi-natural experiment (difference-in-differences design) using data from the Humanitarian OpenStreetMap Team Tasking Manager (HOTTM) platform, a controlled online experiment to test mechanisms, and a changes-in-changes (CIC) analysis for heterogeneous treatment effects. The paper examines how introduction of an AI tool (Rapid) affects volunteer engagement on an online volunteering platform. The main finding is a negative carryover effect of AI introduction on volunteer engagement (21.3% reduction on average), with heterogeneous effects by volunteer experience level: negative for inexperienced volunteers, positive for highly experienced volunteers. The mechanism is theorized to be impact philanthropy—volunteers' motivation to personally make a meaningful difference.

01_extraction.title: AI-Powered Philanthropy: Carryover Effects on Volunteer Engagement
01_extraction.study_design_description: The study adopts a multi-method approach combining a quasi-natural experiment using a difference-in-differences (DID) design and a changes-in-changes (CIC) estimation approach. The quasi-natural experiment emerged during the Turkey-Syria earthquake in February 2023, when the Humanitarian OpenStreetMap Team Tasking Manager (HOTTM) set Rapid (an AI tool) as the default editor for volunteers, creating an exogenous introduction of AI. The adoption period is defined from February 6 to March 7, 2023. The study focuses on carryover effects after the adoption period rather than direct effects during it. A matched sample of 360 treated and 360 control volunteers was constructed using covariate exact matching (CEM), and the full sample of 836 treated and 32,298 control volunteers was used for CIC analysis.
01_extraction.primary_task_description: HOTTM volunteers' primary task is to trace infrastructure (e.g., buildings, roads, waterways) on satellite imagery of areas of interest using a mapping editor. Volunteers log onto HOTTM, select a project, read its description and mapping requirements, then select a task and trace the required infrastructure on satellite imagery. In the online experiment component, participants were asked to create a social media post for an NPO's outreach campaign, an unpaid and optional task where participants view sample posts demonstrating desired content and style, then write their own post to accompany an image about the NPO.
01_extraction.population_description: The study uses two populations: (1) HOTTM volunteers: 836 treated volunteers who used Rapid for the first time during the adoption period and had prior contributions to HOTTM before the adoption period, and 32,298 control volunteers who were active on HOTTM prior to the adoption period (defined as contributing within 431 days of February 6, 2023). The final dataset consists of 836 treated volunteers who performed 151,929 tasks and 32,298 control volunteers who performed 1,156,124 tasks. A matched sample of 360 treated and 360 control volunteers was created using CEM. (2) Online experiment: 600 participants (49.5% female, Mage = 44.2, SDage = 13.5) recruited for the online experiment, with 108 participants (58.3% female, Mage = 46.0, SDage = 14.4) choosing to do the volunteering task.

arms.description: Treated volunteers who used Rapid (an AI tool) for the first time during the adoption period (February 6 to March 7, 2023). Rapid was set as the default editor for volunteers contributing to projects during the Turkey-Syria earthquake response.
Active volunteers who were not introduced to Rapid and contributed to HOTTM prior to the adoption period, defined as contributing within 431 days of February 6, 2023.

In the online experiment, AI-assisted participants had the text box pre-filled with content generated using ChatGPT's GPT-4o free version for writing a social media post, following the same instructions given to participants.
In the online experiment, control participants received an empty text box to write the social media post from scratch.

outcomes.outcome_description: The number of tasks completed on HOTTM by a volunteer in a given month. This serves as a quantitative measure of volunteer participation and engagement on the platform.

Composite score averaging three statements on a 1-7 scale: "Volunteering my time makes a difference in the lives of the kids served by the Educational NPO," "Volunteering for the Educational NPO is an effective way for me to make a difference," and "It is easy for me to imagine how volunteering my time will benefit the Educational NPO." Cronbach's alpha = 0.88.
Composite score averaging two statements on a 1-7 scale: "I put significant effort into helping the Educational NPO" and "I was thorough in helping the Educational NPO." Cronbach's alpha = 0.84.
Number of tasks completed per week by volunteers at different experience percentiles (20th, 30th, 40th, 50th, 60th, 70th, 80th, 90th percentiles), measured after the adoption period.

01_extraction.notes: The study combines a quasi-natural experiment (DID design) using HOTTM platform data with an online controlled experiment and heterogeneous treatment effects analysis (CIC). The main finding is a negative carryover effect of AI introduction on volunteer engagement (21.3% reduction on average), with heterogeneous effects by volunteer experience level: negative for less experienced volunteers (20th-60th percentiles), positive for highly experienced volunteers (80th-90th percentiles). The mechanism is theorized to be impact philanthropy—volunteers' motivation to personally make a meaningful difference. The paper acknowledges potential selection bias since volunteers were not randomly assigned to treatment but rather chose projects that had Rapid set as the default editor; however, 95.6% of treated volunteers used the default editor. The study does not account for the quality of Rapid's AI suggestions or the proportion of time spent using Rapid within tasks. Experience on HOTTM is highly skewed with almost 99% of volunteers having very little experience. When applying CEM matching across multiple variables on small sub-samples, substantial power was lost. Regression coefficients are reported in log-linear form (log of task count as outcome). The online experiment had 108 participants (55 control, 53 AI-assisted) who chose to complete the voluntary social media writing task out of 600 total participants.

02_method.rationale: The study explicitly describes a 'quasi-natural experiment using a difference-in-differences (DID) design and a changes-in-changes (CIC) estimation approach' that emerged when 'HOTTM set Rapid (an AI tool) as the default editor for volunteers, creating an exogenous introduction of AI.' This is a clear quasi-experimental identification strategy comparing treated volunteers who adopted the AI tool to control volunteers who did not, using DID and CIC methods.

per_outcome.operationalization: count of mapping tasks completed by a volunteer on HOTTM platform per month, extracted from platform logs
average of three 1-7 Likert scale items measuring perceived difference-making and benefit to the NPO
average of two 1-7 Likert scale items measuring self-reported effort and thoroughness in helping the NPO
count of mapping tasks completed per week, stratified by volunteer experience percentile groups, from platform logs

04_onet.rationale: The participants' primary task is 'to trace infrastructure (e.g., buildings, roads, waterways) on satellite imagery of areas of interest using a mapping editor' — they log onto HOTTM, select projects, and use digital tools to trace features on satellite imagery. This is fundamentally computer-based work involving digital mapping software and online platforms. While the task involves some visual analysis and decision-making about what to trace, the core activity is operating computer-based mapping tools to digitize geographic features. This maps most directly to 'Working with Computers' rather than more specialized activities like 'Analyzing Data' (which would emphasize interpretation over data entry) or 'Drafting' (which is specific to technical device design, not geographic feature tracing).

llm_extracted.ai_arm_selection_rationale: Single AI intervention: volunteers introduced to Rapid (AI-powered mapping editor) during Turkey-Syria earthquake response. This is the only AI arm in the study.
llm_extracted.notes: Primary outcome is ln(Tasks_it), the log-transformed number of tasks completed by volunteer i in month t. The paper reports a DID coefficient of -0.240*** (SE=0.052) for the Treated_it variable in Table 1. The authors interpret this as 'volunteers reduce their monthly contributions by 21.3% on average' (page 17), which they calculate as e^(-0.240) - 1 = -0.213. The outcome is explicitly log-transformed (larger values = more tasks = better), so the negative coefficient indicates AI introduction reduces volunteer engagement. Sample sizes are from the matched CEM sample: 360 treated and 360 control volunteers (Table EC.2). The regression uses volunteer and month fixed effects with clustered standard errors.
source_quotes: ln(T asksit) = α · T reatedit + β · Xit + δt + γi + ϵit
Volunteers introduced to AI reduce their monthly contributions by 21.3% on average compared to those who were not
Our final dataset consists of 836 treated volunteers who performed 151,929 tasks and 32,298 control volunteers who performed 1,156,124 tasks during the observation window
This matching process leads to a matched sample of 360 treated volunteers and 360 control volunteers
Our dependent variable, T asksit, is operationalized as the number of tasks completed on HOTTM by volunteer i in month t... Since T asksit is highly right-skewed, we log transform this variable

computed.notes: Primary outcome is ln(Tasks_it), the log-transformed number of tasks completed by volunteer i in month t. The paper reports a DID coefficient of -0.240*** (SE=0.052) for the Treated_it variable in Table 1. The authors interpret this as 'volunteers reduce their monthly contributions by 21.3% on average' (page 17), which they calculate as e^(-0.240) - 1 = -0.213. The outcome is explicitly log-transformed (larger values = more tasks = better), so the negative coefficient indicates AI introduction reduces volunteer engagement. Sample sizes are from the matched CEM sample: 360 treated and 360 control volunteers (Table EC.2). The regression uses volunteer and month fixed effects with clustered standard errors. | CI missing; variance not computable

llm_extracted.ai_arm_selection_rationale: The study compares volunteers who were introduced to the AI-powered Rapid editor (treated group) versus those who were not (control group). This is the primary comparison for the main analysis.
llm_extracted.notes: The paper reports a negative carryover effect where volunteers introduced to AI reduce their monthly contributions by 21.3% on average (Table 1: coefficient -0.240, p<0.01). The dependent variable is ln(Tasks_it), which is log-transformed. The paper uses a difference-in-differences design with volunteer and month fixed effects. The matched sample has 360 treated and 360 control volunteers. However, the paper does not report raw means, SDs, or other statistics needed to compute Hedges' g directly. The reported coefficient is from a regression model with fixed effects on a log-transformed outcome, which cannot be directly converted to Hedges' g without additional information. The percentage reduction (21.3%) is calculated as e^(-0.240) - 1. While the paper provides extensive robustness checks and heterogeneity analysis (Tables EC.12-EC.23), none report the raw statistics needed for effect size calculation. The CIC analysis (Table 3) shows quantile treatment effects but these are also not in a form convertible to Hedges' g.
source_quotes: Volunteers introduced to AI reduce their monthly contributions by 21.3% on average compared to those who were not.
Our final dataset consists of 836 treated volunteers who performed 151,929 tasks and 32,298 control volunteers who performed 1,156,124 tasks during the observation window.

By applying CEM, we construct a control group that consists of volunteers who were not introduced to AI but demonstrate comparable engagement and experience on HOTTM to the treated volunteers who were introduced to AI, all measured prior to the adoption period. This matching process leads to a matched sample of 360 treated volunteers and 360 control volunteers.

The dependent variable is ln(Tasks_it), which is log-transformed. The paper uses a difference-in-differences design with volunteer and month fixed effects.
